tool.name: Tag elements
tool.description: from a tabular file of tags

param.name: input
param.label: Input Collection
param.help: The collection whose elements will be tagged.

param.name: tags
param.label: Tag collection elements according to this file

param.name: how
param.label: How should the tags be updated
param.option: New tags will be added, existing tags will be kept
New tags will be added, existing tags will be removed
The tags listed will be removed

has_text_matching.expression: ^This is a different line of text.\n$
^This is a different line of text.\n$

tool.help:
======== Synopsis ========
Assigns tags to collection elements based on a tabular mapping file.
=========== Description ===========
This tool reads a two-column tab-delimited file and applies tags to the matching elements of a collection. The first column contains the element identifier, the second column contains one or more comma-separated tags.
The file does not need to list every element — only elements with a matching identifier will be tagged. Elements not listed keep their existing tags unchanged.
Three tag update modes are available:
- **Add** — new tags are added alongside any existing tags. - **Set** — existing tags are removed and replaced with the new tags. - **Remove** — the listed tags are removed from matching elements.
======== Examples ========
**Add group tags for differential expression**
Given a collection with three samples, and a tag file::
sample1 group:treatment sample2 group:control sample3 group:treatment
After running with mode **Add**, each element will carry the specified group tag while keeping any tags it already had.
-------
.. image:: ${static_path}/images/tools/collection_ops/tag_from_file.svg :alt: Tag collection elements from a tabular file :width: 500
========= Tag types =========
Galaxy supports three types of tags:
**Simple tags**
A plain keyword such as ``mytag`` or ``important``. Simple tags act as labels that help you find and organize datasets in your history. They do not have any special behavior — they are just searchable labels. In the tag file, write the tag name directly::
sample1 important sample2 batch2,rerun
-------
**Name tags**
A tag prefixed with ``#`` or ``name:`` (e.g. ``#patient42`` or ``name:patient42``). Name tags are *hereditary* — when a dataset with a name tag is used as input to a tool, the output dataset automatically inherits the same name tag. This lets you trace the origin of a dataset through a long analysis. In the tag file::
sample1 #patient42 sample2 name:patient99
-------
**Group tags**
A tag prefixed with ``group:`` (e.g. ``group:treatment`` or ``group:control``). Group tags are used by tools that compare conditions — for example, **DESeq2** reads group tags to determine which samples belong to which experimental condition. In the tag file::
sample1 group:treatment sample2 group:control sample3 group:treatment
-------
You can combine multiple tags of any type by separating them with commas::
sample1 #patient42,group:treatment,batch1
-------
To learn more about tags, visit `the Galaxy Training Network`_.
.. _the Galaxy Training Network: https://training.galaxyproject.org/training-material/search?query=tags
-------
.. class:: infomark
@QUOTA_USAGE_NOTE@
